title: wind_driven_wildfire
description: Extreme wind conditions drive a wildfire at dangerous speed, overwhelming standard suppression tactics.

timeline.title: Red Flag Warning Active
timeline.description: National Weather Service issues a Red Flag Warning. Fire weather conditions critical. Stand by.

timeline.title: Ignition Reported
timeline.description: Fire ignites near a power corridor. Wind-driven spread rate is extreme. Request air resources.

timeline.title: Direct Attack Abandoned
timeline.description: Winds make direct attack on fire front impossible. Pivot to indirect tactics.

timeline.title: Mass Evacuation Triggered
timeline.description: Fire moving at 1 mile per hour. Initiate mass evacuation of downwind communities.

timeline.title: Wind Event Moderates
timeline.description: Wind speeds dropping. Window opening for aerial suppression and line construction.

timeline.title: Crews Re-engage
timeline.description: Ground crews safely re-engage as wind subsides. Fire perimeter being established.

node-1.title: Wind Event and Ignition
node-1.situation: A Red Flag Warning is in effect. Sustained winds of 60 mph with gusts over 80 mph are recorded. A fire has ignited and is spreading at extreme rate — up to a mile per hour — in the downwind direction.
node-1.question: What tactics are appropriate under these wind conditions?

options.label: A
options.text: Abandon direct attack, shift to indirect tactics and mass evacuation of downwind communities.

options.label: B
options.text: Commit ground crews to direct attack to stop the fire as quickly as possible.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait for the wind to subside before engaging.

node-2.title: Air Resource Request
node-2.situation: Air tankers are available but the wind conditions create dangerous flying conditions. Pilots are advising caution. Ground resources are positioned safely at a distance.
node-2.question: How do you use air resources?

options.label: A
options.text: Restrict air operations to safe wind thresholds and use aircraft only to drop retardant ahead of the fire to create a buffer.

options.label: B
options.text: Order air tankers to make direct drops on the fire head regardless of wind conditions.

node-3.title: Mass Evacuation Coordination
node-3.situation: Three communities totaling 8,000 residents are in the fire's path. Evacuation routes are limited. Winds are creating dangerous driving conditions with blowing smoke and debris.
node-3.question: How do you manage the mass evacuation safely?

options.label: A
options.text: Issue immediate evacuation orders with specific route assignments to distribute traffic and deploy escorts for vulnerable populations.

options.label: B
options.text: Open all available routes and let residents self-select their exit path.

node-4.title: Wind Subsides — Re-engagement Window
node-4.situation: Wind speeds have dropped significantly. A 2-hour window is projected before conditions deteriorate again. Ground crews and air tankers can now safely engage.
node-4.question: How do you use the re-engagement window?

options.label: A
options.text: Rapidly deploy all available ground and air resources to establish containment lines before winds return.

options.label: B
options.text: Use the window conservatively and position resources for the next wind event rather than aggressively engaging.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: Containment lines were established during the calm window. All communities were evacuated safely. The fire is now 30% contained with favorable conditions for continued suppression.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
